coredata.dc:title: Probing pH variation in living cells and assaying hemoglobin in blood with nitrogen enriched carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, An-Qi
Wang, Ning
Chen, Ming-Li
Shu, Yang
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen enriched carbon dots (CDs) are prepared by hydrothermal treatment with oxalic acid and ethylenediamine serving as raw materials. The obtained CDs display strong blue fluorescence emission at λex/λem = 320/410 nm, along with a quantum yield of 14%. It is interesting to obtain a linear increase of fluorescence with the variation of pH value within pH 5.01–8.94. Meanwhile, a favorable stability of the fluorescence is achieved in a high saline matrix, i.e., up to 1.0 mol L−1 NaCl, making the CDs particularly suitable for probing pH variation in living cells or real biological systems. In addition, hemoglobin (Hb) significantly quenched the fluorescence of CDs due to the inner filter effect and static quenching, providing a linear relationship between fluorescence and Hb concentration within 20–500 mg L−1. The CDs exhibit favorable selectivity for both pH sensing and hemoglobin detection, which are further demonstrated by selective and sensitive intracellular pH monitoring in living HeLa cells and Hb assay in human blood.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen enriched carbon dots
Optical pH sensor
Living cells
Hemoglobin
Assay

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and their pH sensing feature
Intracellular pH sensing in living cells
Fluorescence response feature to hemoglobin and its quantification
Fluorescence feature of the CDs against photobleaching and ionic strength
Cytotoxicity test and cell imaging for pH sensing
Hemoglobin sensing with nitrogen enriched CDs as a probe
Mechanisms of fluorescence response to hemoglobin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images (a) and size distribution histogram (b), AFM image (c) and corresponding height distribution curve (d) of the nitrogen enriched carbon dots.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the nitrogen enriched CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) recorded at various pHs (pH 3.57–11.42). (b) The linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and pH value in the range of 3.57–11.42 (The inset shows a linear calibration graph within pH 5.01–8.94). (c) The fluorescence reversibility of the nitrogen enriched CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) by regulating their solution from pH 3.57 to pH 11.42 and then back to ca. pH 3.57 for 8 repetitive cycles. (d) The dependence of zeta potential of the nitrogen enriched CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) on the variation of pH value.

(a) pH sensing in HeLa cells by confocal fluorescence imaging. HeLa cells are incubated with CDs at pHs 5.37, 6.38, 7.38, 8.34. (b) The linear relationship between pH in HeLa cells and the fluorescence intensity.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the nitrogen enriched CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) aqueous solution in the presence of 0–500 mg L−1 Hb. (b) A linear calibration graph of lg(F
0
/F) versus concentration of Hb.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Hb (500 mg L−1), CDs (1.0 mg mL−1), CDs/Hb (CDs 1.0 mg mL−1, Hb 500 mg L−1) system, the sum of absorbance of CDs and that of Hb and fluorescence emission spectra of CDs at λex/λem = 320/410 nm. (b) The fluorescence decay curves of CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) and CDs/Hb (CDs 1.0 mg mL−1, Hb 500 mg L−1) at λex = 320 nm.
The quantification of hemoglobin in blood samples.

head.ce:title: Probing pH variation in living cells and assaying hemoglobin in blood with nitrogen enriched carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen enriched carbon dots (CDs) are prepared by hydrothermal treatment with oxalic acid and ethylenediamine serving as raw materials. The obtained CDs display strong blue fluorescence emission at λex/λem = 320/410 nm, along with a quantum yield of 14%. It is interesting to obtain a linear increase of fluorescence with the variation of pH value within pH 5.01–8.94. Meanwhile, a favorable stability of the fluorescence is achieved in a high saline matrix, i.e., up to 1.0 mol L−1 NaCl, making the CDs particularly suitable for probing pH variation in living cells or real biological systems. In addition, hemoglobin (Hb) significantly quenched the fluorescence of CDs due to the inner filter effect and static quenching, providing a linear relationship between fluorescence and Hb concentration within 20–500 mg L−1. The CDs exhibit favorable selectivity for both pH sensing and hemoglobin detection, which are further demonstrated by selective and sensitive intracellular pH monitoring in living HeLa cells and Hb assay in human blood.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) is a new class of zero-dimensional photoluminescent nanomaterial with size smaller than 10 nm [1]. CDs generally exhibit favorable solubility in aqueous medium, along with low toxicity, good biocompatibility and photobleaching resistance. A wide range of applications have been reported, e.g., specific metal sensing, drug delivery, cellular imaging and photocatalysis [2–5]. The preparation of CDs can be achieved by either top-down or bottom-up approaches in terms of carbon source.

The pH of living organism usually varies within specific ranges [6] and play critical roles in muscle contraction, tumor growth, cell metabolic, apoptosis and release of ionic species [7–11]. Abnormal pH values in biological cells may give rise to serious diseases, e.g., cancers and Alzheimer's disease [12,13]. Therefore, the monitoring of pH is highly essential for the study of the physiological and pathological processes. For similar purposes, quite a few methodologies have been applied for measuring extracellular and intracellular pH variation, including those based on fluorometry, electrochemistry and nuclear magnetic resonance [14–16]. While in practice the majority of such methodologies are not widely used due to a few limitations, e.g., toxicity, high cost of instrumentations, or complicated operations. In recent years, pH-sensitive CDs derived from various carbon sources have been reported to be effective for pH imaging and sensing in L929 cells, HeLa cells and pathogenic fungal cells [14,17–20]. At the present, however, the sensing of intracellular pH value is greatly hampered due to the complexity of biological matrixes and their interfering effects. In addition, the fluorescence variation may greatly influenced by a lot of parameters, including the bleaching of dyes, the cytotoxicity of the probe itself and the binding of macromolecules in the biological systems. Therefore, further studies on the development of optical pH sensors for the application in biological sample matrixes are highly desired.

Hemoglobin (Hb) is the major protein in red blood cells, which is deeply involved in combining and transportation of oxygen and carbon dioxide. As reported that the normal level of Hb for adult female is generally at 120–160 g L−1, while that for adult male is 130–180 g L−1. Abnormal concentration level of Hb, either significantly higher or lower than the normal level, may cause various diseases, e.g., anemia, cardiovascular and lung disease [21]. Thus, the timely and accurate screening and warning of the Hb level in human body is greatly called for, and this will obviously require reliable analytical or sensing protocols. Recently, carbon dots have been used as fluorescence probe for the detection of various proteins, including hemoglobin, in biological sample matrixes with certain extent of success [22,23]. However, so far it is still highly challenging when complex real biological systems are treated by using such sensing systems or probes.

For the purpose of alleviating the limitations of the above issues encountered for intracellular pH sensing and hemoglobin detection, green or eco-friendly multi-functional carbon dots are highly desired. In the present work, nitrogen enriched blue fluorescence CDs are prepared via a facile and one-pot hydrothermal route with oxalic acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials. It is found that the as-prepared CDs exhibit pH- and hemoglobin-dependent fluorescence emission feature, which make it feasible for intracellular pH sensing and hemoglobin assay, and it is demonstrated to be practical for treating living cells and real biological samples. The mechanisms of the fluorescence quenching of the CDs by hemoglobin are further investigated.

Oxalic acid, ethylenediamine, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, potassium chloride, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, acetic acid, ascorbic acid (AA), glucose, sodium citrate, magnesium chloride, aluminium nitrate, boric acid, phosphoric acid potassium phosphate monobasic, trisodium phosphate anhydrous, sodium hydroxide hydrochloric acid, ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) are purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N-(2-Hydroxyethyl) piperazine-N′-2-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), hemoglobin (Hb), cytochrome c (cyt-c), myoglobin (Mb) are obtained from Sigma-Aldric Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fetal bovine serum, penicillin streptomycin are acquired from KeyGEN Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbeco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), 3-(4,5-dimethyltyiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay kit are the products of VWR Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Nigericin sodium salt is provided by Shanghai Weihuan Biotech Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China).

UV–vis absorption spectra are recorded on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra are recorded by using a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermoelectro, USA). Fluorescence spectra are obtained by using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The quantum yield of CDs is measured with an absolute PL Quantum yield spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Japan). Fluorescence decay curves are obtained by using a FLS980 series of fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, England). pH measurement in aqueous medium is performed with a PB-10 pH-meter (Beijing Sartorious Instruments Co. LTD, China). Atomic force microscope (AFM) imaging is performed on a Dimension Icon ScanAsyst atomic force microscope (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra are recorded on an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images are recorded by using a JEM-ARM 200F (HR) transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells is conducted with a FV1200 confocal fluorescent microscope (Olympus, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and their pH sensing feature
ce:section.ce:para: 0.3 g of oxalic acid and 475 μL of ethylenediamine are mixed with 10 mL of deionized water. The mixture is then set into a 25 mL Teflon-lined pressure vessel and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the brown solution is taken out and concentrated by rotary evaporation followed by purification with Sephadex G-25 gel filtration chromatography. The CDs are obtained by freeze drying and they are afterwards dispersed into deionized water to produce a stock solution of 100 mg mL−1 which is stored at −4 °C for future use. The variation of fluorescence intensity of CDs solution with pH is thoroughly investigated. Briefly, the Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solution is prepared with acetic acid (0.04 mol L−1), boric acid (0.04 mol L−1) and phosphoric acid (0.04 mol L−1), and pH value of the BR buffer is adjusted to 3.57, 3.98, 4.57, 5.01, 5.62, 6.02, 6.49 7.00, 7.61, 7.97, 8.49, 8.94, 9.43, 9.95, 10.56, 11.00 and 11.42 with 0.2 mol L−1 of sodium hydroxide solution. Then, 10 μL of CDs solution (100 mg mL−1) is diluted to 1.0 mL with the corresponding BR buffer. The fluorescence emission spectra of the diluted CDs solutions are then recorded at λex = 320 nm. For the purpose of further exploiting the fluorescence reversibility of the nitrogen enriched CDs, their solution is adjusted from pH 3.57 to pH 11.42 with sodium hydroxide solution, and then adjusted back to ca. pH 3.57 with hydrochloric acid solution. This operation is repeated for eight cycles and the fluorescence emission spectrum at each pH value is recorded for evaluation.

HeLa cells are firstly cultured in DMEM medium containing 1% penicillin and streptomycin, 10% fetal bovine serum with 5% CO2, at 37 °C. The biocompatibility of the obtained CDs is thereafter evaluated by measuring the cell viability with MTT assays. HeLa cells are seeded into 96-well plates incubated for 24 h followed by washing for twice with PBS at pH 7.4. Subsequently, different concentrations of CDs, e.g., 0.025, 0.05, 0.075, 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75 and 1.0 mg mL−1, are added into the wells and incubated with HeLa cells for 20 h. Afterwards, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg mL−1) are added and further incubated with HeLa cells for another 4 h to form bluish violet formazan crystals. After removing the medium, 150 μL of DMSO are added into each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. Finally, the absorbance at 490 nm is recorded for the reaction mixture in each well by ELISA plate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular pH sensing in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular pH sensing capacity of CDs is evaluated by using confocal fluorescent microscope. After incubating with 0.75 mg mL−1 of CDs for 7 h, HeLa cells are washed for twice with PBS at pH 7.4. Then the cells are treated with high K+ HEPES buffer solutions (125 mM KCl, 20 mM NaCl, 0.5 mM CaCl2, 0.5 mM MgCl2, 5 mM glucose, 20 mM HEPES) at different pHs (5.37, 6.38, 7.38 and 8.34) containing 0.5 μg mL−1 nigericin for 30 min. Finally, the fluorescence images of the cells are taken under confocal fluorescent microscope at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. For the purpose of pH quantification, a linear calibration graph is achieved between pH value and the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response feature to hemoglobin and its quantification
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response nature of the CDs to hemoglobin is thoroughly investigated by mixing a series of Hb solutions (within the range of 0–1000 μg mL−1) with 10 μL of CDs solution (at a concentration level of 100 mg mL−1). The mixture is then shaken with vortex mixer for 5 min and subsequently subject to fluorescence measurement at λex/λem = 320 nm/405 nm. The linear calibration graph between the fluorescence intensity and Hb concentration is thereafter derived for the purpose of quantification.

The practical application for hemoglobin detection in real biological samples is demonstrated by quantifying Hb in red cells. Briefly, 1.0 mL of blood sample is centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min for collecting the red cells, which are then diluted with 4 mL of deionized water for bursting the red cells and releasing hemoglobin. The supernatant is collected by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 20 min. The treated blood samples are stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. The stock blood samples are diluted for 250-fold with 10 mmol L−1 PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4) before fluorescence detection.

The nitrogen enriched CDs are characterized by means of HRTEM image, AFM image, FT-IR spectra and XPS spectra. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, HRTEM image indicates that the CDs are approximately spherical and the diameter distribution is located within the range of 3–5.5 nm with an average diameter of 4.3 nm as illustrated in Fig. 1b. AFM image in Fig. 1c demonstrates a height distribution of 2.5–4.5 nm for the CDs with an average height of 3.9 nm (Fig. 1d), this observation is well consistent with that observed in the HRTEM image.

FT-IR spectrum of the obtained CDs is shown in Fig. S1 with that of oxalic acid for comparison. The broad absorption band at 3250 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H groups, while that at 1675 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C˭O group. In addition, the absorption at 1591 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of N-H group. It is seen that in comparison with the absorption at 1737 cm−1 of C˭O group in oxalic acid, that observed for the CDs exhibits an obvious red shift, attributed to the conjugation between the lone pair electrons on nitrogen and the carbonyl groups in amino group. These ovations clearly indicated that -COOH, -NH2 and -CONH groups exist on the surface of the obtained CDs, which enable the CDs favorable water solubility.

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) analyses are used to describe the elemental compositions and the surface functional groups of the CDs. Three typical peaks of C1s (285 eV), N1s (400 eV) and O1s (531 eV) are identified in the XPS spectrum (Fig. S2a), and the elemental contents of C, N and O are derived to be 67.84, 14.30, 17.86 wt%, respectively. The high resolution spectrum of C1s exhibits three peaks at 284.64 eV, 286.02 eV and 287.98 eV, which are assigned to C-C/C˭C, C–O/C–N and C˭O bonds (Fig. S2b). Two peaks at 399.81 eV and 401.10 eV in the high resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. S2c) are attributed to pyrrolic nitrogen (C-N-C) and alkyl ammonium nitrogen (C-NH2). The O1s spectrum displays two peaks. The main peak at 531.46 eV is assigned to C–OH/C–O–C bond, while the small peak at 532.43 eV corresponds to C˭O bond (Fig. S2d). XPS analysis results, along with the observations illustrated by FT-IR spectra, demonstrate the presence of -COOH, -NH2 and -CONH groups on the surface of the CDs.

The optical properties of CDs are thereafter thoroughly investigated. UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CDs as shown in Fig. S3a indicates a strong absorption at 212 nm, which is attributed to π-π* transition of the conjugated C˭C bonds, and similarly the absorption band at around 278–282 nm is assigned to n-π* transition of C˭O bond. A series of fluorescence spectra for the CDs are recorded by varying the excitation wavelength from 260 nm to 440 nm (Fig. S3b). The fluorescence emission wavelength of CDs is found to be excitation wavelength-dependent. This is generally attributed to “defect stated” with a broad energy distribution on graphitized core of the CDs [24]. The maximum fluorescence emission is observed at 410 nm with an excitation wavelength at 320 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence feature of the CDs against photobleaching and ionic strength
ce:section.ce:para: For practical use as an optical probe, the resistance of CDs fluorescence against irradiation time and the ionic strength in the reaction medium is highly important. Thus, corresponding experiments are conducted for further evaluation of the CDs fluorescence property, as illustrated in Fig. S4. A favorable photobleaching stability for the CDs fluorescence is observed, as given in Fig. S4a, where the fluorescence intensity at λex/λem = 320 nm/410 nm remains virtually unchanged after a continuous irradiation for 120 min under a Xe lamp with power rating at 150 w, and actually only a very slight decrease of ca. 3.5% is encountered. On the other hand, the variation of fluorescence intensity at various ionic strength, i.e., in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl (0–1.0 mol L−1), is investigated and illustrated in Fig. S4b. It is seen that no effect on the fluorescence of the nitrogen enriched CDs is observed within the NaCl concentration range studied, indicating favorable stability of the fluorescence in saline sample matrix, and this feature is particularly important for treating real biological samples, where high salinity is generally encountered.

The presence of a large number of -COOH and -NH2 groups on the nitrogen doped CDs make it possible to exhibit sensitive and rapid response to the variation of pH value in the aqueous medium, and thus provide a potential approach for pH sensing. Fig. 2a illustrated an obvious increase on the fluorescence intensity of CDs with the increase of pH value. A linear correlation is achieved between the fluorescence intensity of the CDs and the pH value within a range of 5.01–8.94, as given in the inset of Fig. 2b, with a linear calibration graph of F = 402.42pH + 394.7. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen enriched CDs shows excellent reversibility and stability when pH value of their aqueous solution is alternatively switched between 3.57 and 11.42 for 8 cycles, as illustrated in Fig. 2c.

The pH-dependent feature of carbon dots is associated to the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups on the surface of the CDs, e.g., amino groups and carboxyl groups in the present case. It was reported that similar to the pH-dependent single-walled carbon nanotubes, the protonation and deprotonation of the functional groups could cause electrostatic doping/charging to the CDs and therefore shift the Fermi level [18,25–28]. In addition, the deprotonation of amino groups and hydroxyl groups would passivate the defects on the surface of the CDs and thus gradually create a “protective shell” with negative charge. The “protective shell” can therefore make the CDs isolated and lower down the non-radiative recombination rate, which may contribute to the enhancement on the quantum yield of CDs [29,30]. However, the fluorescence intensities of some CDs decrease with the increasing pH values. The noncovalent molecular interactions between the CDs are weak at higher pH and the energy loss through the nonradiative rotational relaxation channel increases, which lead to the decrement of fluorescence intensity [31,32]. The opposite variation of fluorescence intensity on pH may result from the different surface structure of CDs.

As for the present CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs increases with pH variation from 3.57 to 11.42. To further illustrate the pH sensitive feature of the present nitrogen enriched CDs, zeta potential of CDs at various pH values are investigated. It is found that zeta potential changes from 2.37 mV to −17.07 mV with pH variation from 4 to 11 (Fig. 2d). The results illustrated that the surface of CDs is highly sensitive to pH, which further demonstrated that the abundant groups on the surface of the nitrogen enriched CDs, e.g., amide groups and hydroxyl groups, make it highly feasible for protonation and deprotonation of the CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity test and cell imaging for pH sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of the nitrogen enriched CDs should be first evaluated before they are applied for cell imaging tests. The viability of HeLa cells after incubation with various concentrations of nitrogen enriched CDs is calculated by MTT test (Fig. S5). It is indicated that the viability of HeLa cells remains > 91% as the concentration of CDs increases up to 1.0 mg mL−1. That is, the nitrogen enriched CDs exhibit favorable biocompatibility and very low cytotoxicity, which provide a promising platform for the treatment of real biological systems, including imaging in living cells.

For further demonstrating the feasibility of the nitrogen enriched CDs as a fluorescence probe for pH sensing in real biological systems, pH monitoring in living cells is investigated. The cell imaging results after incubating HeLa cells with as-prepared CDs are illustrated in Fig. S6. The fluorescence intensity of HeLa cells increases with the incubation time up to 7 h. This is probably due to gradual accumulation of CDs into the cytoplasm. A decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed After 11 h, which might be due to the excretion of CDs out of the cells by metabolism. So an incubation time of 7 h was selected for this study. As described in the experimental section, HeLa cells are incubated with 0.75 mg mL−1 of CDs and treated with high K+ HEPES buffer containing 0.5 μg mL−1 nigericin for 30 min. Nigericin is a kind of polycyclic ether carboxylic acid compound, it is used to improve the permeability of cell membrane and therefore to approximately equilibrate the intracellular and extracellular pH. As shown in Fig. 3a, the fluorescence images are acquired with excitation at 405 nm, 488 nm and 559 nm, respectively. It is obvious that the brightness of HeLa cells gradually increased with intracellular pH value. For the purpose of quantitative sensing, the mean fluorescence intensity in living HeLa cells is recorded and analyzed by using Image J software. Fig. 3b illustrated a linear calibration curve of fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen enriched CDs versus intracellular pH value, as is expressed in the following equation with a correlation coefficient of 0.9933. This provides a facile approach for the quantification of intracellular pH value in living cells. F = 0.7330pH+ 22.81.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemoglobin sensing with nitrogen enriched CDs as a probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The sensing performance of the nitrogen enriched CDs for Hb in aqueous solution is subsequently investigated. The CDs are mixed with different concentrations of Hb and their fluorescence spectra in the presence of various amount of Hb are shown in Fig. 4a. The fluorescence intensity of CDs at λex/λem = 320 nm/405 nm is found to decrease significantly with the increase of Hb concentration. It is obviously seen that the emission peak of CDs is quite broad with a span of 330–575 nm. On the other hand, however, the absorption band of Hb is relatively narrower with approximately a peak width of ca. 100 nm (Fig. 4a), which leads to incomplete quenching of CDs fluorescence. This is clearly reflected in the fluorescence emission spectrum in Fig. 4a, where it exhibits two peaks, with one keeps blue-shifting and the other keeps red-shifting with the increase of Hb concentration. In practice, the fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen enriched CDs/Hb solution at 405 nm is recorded for the quantification of Hb. A linear relationship is obtained between the value of lg(F
0
/F) and the concentration of Hb within a range of 20–500 mg L−1 (Fig. 4b). The calibration curve is expressed as lg(F
0
/F) = 10−3CHb + 0.018, where F
0 and F denote the fluorescence intensity of the nitrogen enriched CDs in the absence and/or in the presence of Hb respectively. A detection limit of 5.04 mg L−1 (78.1 nmol L−1) is derived for the quantification of Hb in aqueous medium.

In biological sample matrixes, the coexisting of abundant proteins, metal species and organic molecules may cause serious interferences for the detection of hemoglobin. For evaluating the selectivity to hemoglobin by assay with the nitrogen enriched CDs as probe, the potential interfering effects of some ionic species and molecules are thoroughly investigated. These species include 1 mM of ascorbic acid, glucose, EDTA, Ca2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Na+, K+, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2- and PO4 3-, 1 μM of cyt-c and Mb. On the other hand, when discussing pH sensing performance with the nitrogen enriched CDs as probe, the fluorescence intensity of CDs changes significantly with the variation of pH value. Therefore, the effects of the above mentioned species on the quantification of Hb are further investigated at pHs 6.0, 7.0, 8.0 and 9.0, and the results are shown in Fig. S7. It is clearly demonstrated that these species cause virtually no influence on the fluorescence ratio of F
0
/F at the pH values tested, and thus have no interference on the detection of hemoglobin. These observations clearly indicated that the sensing platform with the nitrogen enriched CDs as probe exhibits favorable selectivity to hemoglobin.

Hemoglobin in human blood samples are detected by using the above mentioned fluorescence approach with nitrogen enriched CDs as probe. The blood samples are first processed as described in the Section 2, and the contents of hemoglobin are then quantified. The contents of Hb in blood samples 1 and 2 are derived to be 120 ± 4 mg L−1 and 133 ± 7 mg L−1, respectively. Afterwards, spiking recoveries are performed to further demonstrate the accuracy of the present assay approach. Table 1 illustrated that satisfactory results are achieved for both blood samples at various concentration levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of fluorescence response to hemoglobin
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, fluorescence quenching mechanisms include static quenching, dynamic quenching, inner filter effect (IFE) and energy transfer [33],. The UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence decay curves are then recorded for the illustration of quenching mechanisms of the fluorescence of the nitrogen enriched CDs by hemoglobin. As depicted in Fig. 5a, the absorption peak of Hb in the UV–vis region centered at 405 nm (black line) is significantly overlapped with the emission peak in the fluorescence spectra of CDs (green line). This suggests that IFE or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) might be involved in the CDs/Hb fluorescence system. In addition, the absorption of CDs/Hb system at 405 nm (orange line) exhibits a tiny decrease in comparison with the sum of absorbance of CDs and that of Hb (blue line). It rules out the possibility of dynamic quenching of CDs fluorescence by Hb, because dynamic quenching only affects the excited states of the CDs and thus the absorption spectra of CDs would not change [33]. This result indicates that static quenching might exist in this system.

The quenching mechanism is further investigated with fluorescence decay curves (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is measured to be 7.5221 ns, and no obvious change of fluorescence lifetime (7.2741 ns) is present after the addition of Hb. This observation clearly eliminates the possibility of FRET mechanism, because non-radiative energy transfer is expected to essentially alter the exciton lifetime of the donor [34]. Overall, the experiments well demonstrate that IFE and static quenching give rise to the fluorescence quenching of the nitrogen enriched CDs by hemoglobin.

A novel nitrogen enriched carbon dots is prepared by a facile one-step approach with oxalic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors without further modification. The CDs are found to exhibit pH-dependent fluorescent emission, which provide a fluorescent probe for intracellular pH sensing and living cell imaging. In addition, the CDs offer a clear response to the fluorescence emission in the presence of hemoglobin, it thus provide a selective sensor probe for Hb detection based on the inner filter effect and static quenching. The present study provides an avenue for the development of novel probing platform/system for biological sensing purposes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21575020, 21727811, 21675019), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N152004005, N160302001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.06.048.
